content:
# Numbers Chapter 25 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 178–179 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_025_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_025_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0132 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_025_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE ZEAL OF PHINEHAS AND THE PLAGUE AT BAAL PEOR:** Numbers 25 narrates one of the gravest crises in Israel's wilderness journey: the seduction of Israel into the worship of Baal of Peor through the women of Moab and Midian. The chapter divides into three sections: (1) the apostasy and divine judgment (vv. 1–5), in which Israel yokes itself to Baal Peor and YHWH commands Moses to impale the leaders; (2) the zealous act of Phinehas (vv. 6–9), who drives a spear through an Israelite man and a Midianite woman in the tent-shrine, halting the plague that killed twenty-four thousand; and (3) the divine reward and identification of the slain (vv. 10–18), in which YHWH grants Phinehas a covenant of peace and an eternal priesthood, and the chapter closes with YHWH commanding hostility against the Midianites. In the Leningrad Codex, Numbers 25 spans pages 178–179, with the text appearing in the characteristic three-column format with Masoretic marginalia. The parashah break between Balak (ending at 25:9) and Phinehas (beginning at 25:10) falls within this chapter — a striking literary feature, as the narrative of Phinehas's act is deliberately split across two parashot. The Vaticanus LXX (pa_0132) preserves the chapter with close fidelity to the MT, though notable divergences include the naming of "Salpaad" in place of certain names and minor syntactic variations. The Hebrew בַּעַל פְּעוֹר (Ba'al Pe'or) denotes the local Moabite deity associated with Mount Peor — likely a fertility cult involving ritual sexual acts, which explains the intertwining of sexual and cultic transgression throughout the narrative.

---

## The Apostasy at Shittim (vv. 1–5)

**1** And-dwelt [va-yeshev] Israel [Yisra'el] in-Shittim [ba-Shittim]; and-began [va-yakhel] the-people [ha-am] to-commit-harlotry [liznot] unto [el] the-daughters of [benot] Moab [Mo'av].

> **Key terms:** וַיֵּשֶׁב (va-yeshev) = "and-he/it-dwelt" — from ישׁב (yashav, "to sit/dwell/remain"). The location הַשִּׁטִּים (ha-Shittim) = "the Acacias" — the last encampment before crossing the Jordan, situated in the plains of Moab opposite Jericho (cf. Josh 2:1; 3:1). וַיָּחֶל (va-yakhel) = "and-began" — from חלל (khalal, "to begin/profane"); the hiphil form carries the sense of initiating an action, but the root's secondary meaning "to profane/defile" creates a deliberate double-entendre. לִזְנוֹת (liznot) = "to commit harlotry" — the infinitive construct of זנה (zanah), used throughout the prophets for both literal sexual immorality and metaphorical spiritual apostasy. The object — אֶל בְּנוֹת מוֹאָב (el benot Mo'av, "unto the daughters of Moab") — introduces the foreign women as agents of seduction, linking sexual transgression to the cultic apostasy that follows in v. 2. **LXX:** καὶ κατέλυσεν Ισραηλ ἐν Σαττιν, καὶ ἐβεβηλώθη ὁ λαὸς ἐκπορνεῦσαι εἰς τὰς θυγατέρας Μωαβ ("and Israel encamped at Sattin, and the people was profaned to commit fornication with the daughters of Moab") — notably, the LXX renders וַיָּחֶל with ἐβεβηλώθη ("was profaned"), choosing the "profane" rather than "begin" sense of חלל.

**2** And-they-called [va-tiqre'nah] to-the-people [la-am] to-the-sacrifices of [le-zivkhei] their-gods [eloheihen]; and-ate [va-yokhal] the-people [ha-am] and-bowed-down [va-yishtakhavu] to-their-gods [le-eloheihen].

> **Key terms:** וַתִּקְרֶאןָ (va-tiqre'nah) = "and-they-called" (feminine plural) — the subject is the daughters of Moab from v. 1; the feminine verb-form makes the women the active agents of the seduction. The sequence is precise: invitation (קרא, qara') → eating the sacrificial meal (אכל, akhal) → bowing down in worship (השׁתחוה, hishtakhavah). The eating of sacrificial meat constitutes participation in pagan worship (cf. Exod 34:15; 1 Cor 10:18–20). אֱלֹהֵיהֶן (eloheihen) = "their gods" (feminine suffix) — the gods of the Moabite women. **LXX:** καὶ ἐκάλεσαν αὐτοὺς ἐπὶ ταῖς θυσίαις τῶν εἰδώλων αὐτῶν, καὶ ἔφαγεν ὁ λαὸς τῶν θυσιῶν αὐτῶν καὶ προσεκύνησαν τοῖς εἰδώλοις αὐτῶν ("and they called them to the sacrifices of their idols, and the people ate of their sacrifices and worshipped their idols") — the LXX renders אֱלֹהֵיהֶן as εἰδώλων/εἰδώλοις ("idols"), an interpretive shift from the neutral Hebrew "gods."

**3** And-yoked-himself [va-yitsamed] Israel [Yisra'el] to-Baal of [le-Ba'al] Peor [Pe'or]; and-burned [va-yikhar] the-anger of [af] YHWH against-Israel [be-Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּצָּמֶד (va-yitsamed) = "and-he-yoked-himself" — the niphal of צמד (tsamad, "to bind/yoke/attach"), depicting Israel's attachment to the Baal cult as a beast yoked to its master. This verb recurs in the retrospective of Ps 106:28 and Deut 4:3. בַּעַל פְּעוֹר (Ba'al Pe'or) = "Lord/Master of Peor" — the local deity of the Moabite site on Mount Peor (cf. Num 23:28). וַיִּחַר אַף יְהוָה (va-yikhar af YHWH) = "and-burned the-nose/anger of YHWH" — the standard idiom for divine wrath. אַף (af) literally means "nose," evoking the flaring of nostrils in fury. **LXX:** καὶ ἐτελέσθη Ισραηλ τῷ Βεελφεγωρ ("and Israel was initiated to Beelphegor") — the LXX renders וַיִּצָּמֶד with ἐτελέσθη ("was initiated/consecrated"), interpreting the yoking as a cultic initiation into the rites of Baal Peor.

**4** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], "Take [qakh] [et] all [kol] the-heads of [rashei] the-people [ha-am], and-hang-them [ve-hoqa' otam] before-YHWH [la-YHWH] against [neged] the-sun [ha-shamesh]; and-shall-turn-back [ve-yashov] the-burning of [kharon] the-anger of [af] YHWH from-Israel [mi-Yisra'el]."

> **Key terms:** וְהוֹקַע אוֹתָם (ve-hoqa' otam) = "and-hang/impale them" — from יקע (yaqa', hiphil), meaning to publicly expose or impale. The precise method of execution is debated: the verb may indicate hanging, impaling on a stake, or dislocating/dismembering. The Targum Onkelos renders it as "crucify" (צְלוֹב). The purpose is public: נֶגֶד הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ (neged ha-shamesh) = "against/before the sun" — i.e., in broad daylight, in public view. רָאשֵׁי הָעָם (rashei ha-am) = "the heads of the people" — this could mean the ringleaders (those who led the apostasy) or the tribal chiefs (held responsible for their people's conduct). The Mishnah (Sanhedrin 35a) interprets it as the judges who failed to restrain the people. **LXX:** λαβὲ πάντας τοὺς ἀρχηγοὺς τοῦ λαοῦ καὶ παραδειγμάτισον αὐτοὺς κυρίῳ ἀπέναντι τοῦ ἡλίου ("take all the leaders of the people and make an example of them before the Lord in the face of the sun") — rendering וְהוֹקַע as παραδειγμάτισον ("make a public example"), emphasizing the deterrent purpose.

**5** And-said [va-yomer] Moses [Mosheh] unto [el] the-judges of [shoftei] Israel [Yisra'el], "Kill [hirgu], each-man [ish] [et] his-men [anashav], the-ones-yoked [ha-nitsmadim] to-Baal of [le-Ba'al] Peor [Pe'or]."

> **Key terms:** שֹׁפְטֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (shoftei Yisra'el) = "the judges of Israel" — Moses delegates the execution to the judicial officials rather than carrying it out personally. הַנִּצְמָדִים (ha-nitsmadim) = "the ones yoked" — the niphal participle of צמד, the same root as v. 3, now used to identify the specific guilty parties. אִישׁ אֲנָשָׁיו (ish anashav) = "each man his men" — each judge is responsible for executing the offenders under his jurisdiction. **LXX:** ἀποκτείνατε ἕκαστος τὸν οἰκεῖον αὐτοῦ τὸν τετελεσμένον τῷ Βεελφεγωρ ("kill each his own kinsman who has been initiated to Beelphegor") — again using τετελεσμένον ("initiated") for the yoking-language.

---

## The Zeal of Phinehas (vv. 6–9)

**6** And-behold [ve-hinneh], a-man [ish] from-the-Sons of [mi-benei] Israel [Yisra'el] came [ba] and-brought-near [va-yaqrev] unto [el] his-brothers [ekhav] [et] the-Midianite-woman [ha-Midyanit], before-the-eyes of [le-einei] Moses [Mosheh] and-before-the-eyes of [u-le-einei] all [kol] the-congregation of [adat] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el]; and-they [ve-hemmah] were-weeping [bokhim] at-the-entrance of [petakh] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּקְרֵב (va-yaqrev) = "and-he-brought-near" — from קרב (qarav, hiphil), a verb loaded with sacrificial connotation: to "bring near" is the standard verb for presenting an offering. The man brings the Midianite woman as one might bring a sacrifice — a deliberate, brazen affront. הַמִּדְיָנִית (ha-Midyanit) = "the Midianite woman" — note the shift from "daughters of Moab" in v. 1 to a Midianite woman here; the chapter weaves Moabite and Midianite involvement together (resolved in v. 18). לְעֵינֵי מֹשֶׁה (le-einei Mosheh) = "before the eyes of Moses" — the act is performed in full view of Moses and the entire weeping congregation, an act of extraordinary public defiance. בֹּכִים (bokhim) = "weeping" — the congregation is weeping, presumably in penitential grief or in response to the plague already underway. **LXX:** καὶ ἰδοὺ ἄνθρωπος τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ ἐλθὼν προσήγαγεν τὸν ἀδελφὸν αὐτοῦ πρὸς τὴν Μαδιανῖτιν ἐναντίον Μωυσῆ καὶ ἔναντι πάσης συναγωγῆς υἱῶν Ισραηλ· αὐτοὶ δὲ ἔκλαιον παρὰ τὴν θύραν τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("and behold, a man of the sons of Israel came and brought his brother to the Midianite woman before Moses and before all the assembly of the sons of Israel; and they were weeping by the door of the tent of testimony").

**7** And-saw [va-yar'] Phinehas [Pinekhas] son of [ben] Eleazar [El'azar] son of [ben] Aaron [Aharon] the-priest [ha-kohen], and-he-rose [va-yaqom] from-the-midst of [mi-tokh] the-congregation [ha-edah], and-he-took [va-yiqqakh] a-spear [romakh] in-his-hand [be-yado].

> **Key terms:** פִּינְחָס בֶּן אֶלְעָזָר בֶּן אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֵן (Pinekhas ben El'azar ben Aharon ha-kohen) = "Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron the priest" — the full genealogy establishes Phinehas as the grandson of Aaron, third generation of the priestly line. רֹמַח (romakh) = "a spear/lance" — a thrusting weapon distinct from the more common חֲנִית (khanit). The romakh appears rarely in the Torah (only here and in 1 Kgs 18:28 in a cultic context). וַיָּקׇם מִתּוֹךְ הָעֵדָה (va-yaqom mi-tokh ha-edah) = "and he rose from the midst of the congregation" — Phinehas separates himself from the weeping assembly to act. **LXX:** καὶ ἰδὼν Φινεες υἱὸς Ελεαζαρ υἱοῦ Ααρων τοῦ ἱερέως ἐξανέστη ἐκ μέσου τῆς συναγωγῆς καὶ λαβὼν σειρομάστην ἐν τῇ χειρὶ αὐτοῦ ("and Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron the priest, seeing [it], rose up from the midst of the assembly and took a javelin in his hand") — rendering רֹמַח as σειρομάστην ("javelin/lance").

**8** And-he-went [va-yavo'] after [akhar] the-man [ish] of-Israel [Yisra'el] into [el] the-tent-chamber [ha-qubbah], and-he-thrust [va-yidqor] [et] both-of-them [sheneihem], [et] the-man [ish] of-Israel [Yisra'el] and [ve-et] the-woman [ha-ishah] unto [el] her-belly [qovatah]; and-was-restrained [va-te'atser] the-plague [ha-maggefah] from-upon [me-al] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** הַקֻּבָּה (ha-qubbah) = "the tent-chamber" — a hapax legomenon (occurring only here in the Hebrew Bible). The word likely refers to a vaulted tent or inner chamber, perhaps a cultic tent-shrine. Some scholars connect it to Arabic qubbah ("domed tent/pavilion"). וַיִּדְקֹר (va-yidqor) = "and-he-thrust/pierced" — from דקר (daqar, "to pierce through"), indicating a forceful thrust that penetrated both bodies. אֶל קֳבָתָהּ (el qovatah) = "unto her belly/abdomen" — from קֵבָה (qevah, "stomach/belly"), which may also carry anatomical specificity suggesting the act interrupted a sexual encounter. The phonetic resonance between קֻּבָּה (qubbah, "tent-chamber") and קֳבָתָהּ (qovatah, "her belly") appears deliberate — a grim wordplay linking the place and the wound. הַמַּגֵּפָה (ha-maggefah) = "the plague" — from נגף (nagaf, "to strike/smite"); this is the first mention that a plague was already in progress among the people. **LXX:** καὶ εἰσῆλθεν ὀπίσω τοῦ ἀνθρώπου τοῦ Ισραηλίτου εἰς τὴν κάμινον καὶ ἀπεκέντησεν ἀμφοτέρους, τόν τε ἄνθρωπον τὸν Ισραηλίτην καὶ τὴν γυναῖκα διὰ τῆς μήτρας αὐτῆς ("and he went in after the Israelite man into the chamber and pierced both of them, the Israelite man and the woman through her womb") — the LXX renders קֻּבָּה as κάμινον ("furnace/vault") and קֳבָתָהּ as μήτρας ("womb"), making the anatomical meaning explicit.

**9** And-were [va-yihyu] the-ones-dying [ha-metim] in-the-plague [ba-maggefah] four [arba'ah] and-twenty [ve-esrim] thousand ['alef].

> **Key terms:** אַרְבָּעָה וְעֶשְׂרִים אָלֶף (arba'ah ve-esrim alef) = "four and twenty thousand" — i.e., twenty-four thousand dead. This number is noteworthy in comparison to 1 Cor 10:8, where Paul cites "twenty-three thousand" (τρεῖς καὶ εἴκοσι χιλιάδες). The discrepancy has been harmonized by various means: that Paul counted only those who fell in one day, or that the thousand-unit difference reflects those impaled by the judges (v. 4–5) vs. those struck by the plague proper. **Parashah break:** The Masoretic tradition places the parashah division here — Parashat Balak ends at v. 9, and Parashat Pinekhas begins at v. 10. This means the dramatic act of Phinehas (vv. 6–8) is read as the climax of the Balak reading, while the divine reward (vv. 10–13) opens the next week's reading — a powerful liturgical arrangement that lets the act of zeal linger before its reward is disclosed. **LXX:** καὶ ἐγένοντο οἱ τεθνηκότες ἐν τῇ πληγῇ τέσσαρες καὶ εἴκοσι χιλιάδες ("and those who died in the plague were four and twenty thousand").

---

## The Covenant of Peace for Phinehas (vv. 10–15)

**10** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

> **Parashah Pinekhas begins here** (פ — open section in the Leningrad Codex). YHWH now speaks to commend and reward the act narrated in the previous parashah.

**11** "Phinehas [Pinekhas] son of [ben] Eleazar [El'azar] son of [ben] Aaron [Aharon] the-priest [ha-kohen] turned-back [heshiv] [et] my-wrath [khamati] from-upon [me-al] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], in-his-being-zealous [be-qan'o] [et] my-zeal [qin'ati] in-their-midst [be-tokham]; and-not [ve-lo] I-consumed [khilliti] [et] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] in-my-zeal [be-qin'ati]."

> **Key terms:** הֵשִׁיב אֶת חֲמָתִי (heshiv et khamati) = "turned back my wrath" — the hiphil of שׁוב (shuv) with חֵמָה (khemah, "heat/wrath"). Phinehas's act serves as a substitutionary turning-away of divine fury. בְּקַנְאוֹ אֶת קִנְאָתִי (be-qan'o et qin'ati) = "in his being zealous [with] my zeal" — the infinitive construct + cognate accusative creates an **infinitive absolute-like intensification**: Phinehas's zeal was not his own but God's own jealousy channeled through him. The root קנא (qana') carries both "jealousy" and "zeal" — a passionate, exclusive devotion that cannot tolerate rivals. וְלֹא כִלִּיתִי (ve-lo khilliti) = "and-not I-consumed" — from כלה (kalah, "to complete/finish/consume"), in the piel "to destroy utterly." YHWH's statement implies that without Phinehas's intervention, the entire nation would have been annihilated. **LXX:** Φινεες υἱὸς Ελεαζαρ υἱοῦ Ααρων τοῦ ἱερέως κατέπαυσεν τὸν θυμόν μου ἀπὸ υἱῶν Ισραηλ ἐν τῷ ζηλῶσαί μου τὸν ζῆλον ἐν αὐτοῖς ("Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron the priest caused my fury to cease from the sons of Israel in his being zealous with my zeal among them").

**12** "Therefore [lakhen] say [emor]: 'Behold-me [hineni] giving [noten] to-him [lo] [et] my-covenant of [beriti] peace [shalom].'"

> **Key terms:** בְּרִיתִי שָׁלוֹם (beriti shalom) = "my covenant of peace" — the most remarkable reward in the passage. שָׁלוֹם (shalom) here carries its fullest sense: wholeness, well-being, completeness, harmony. The paradox is striking — a covenant of peace granted for an act of violence. The Masoretic tradition preserves a distinctive scribal feature: in many manuscripts (including the Leningrad Codex), the ו (vav) in שָׁלוֹם is written as a "broken vav" (וָו קְטִיעָא) — a vav with a visible break or gap in its vertical stroke. The rabbinic tradition (Talmud Bavli, Qiddushin 66b) debated the significance of this broken letter, some seeing it as an indication that the peace given was itself "broken" or incomplete in some way. **LXX:** διὰ τοῦτο οὕτως εἰπόν· ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ δίδωμι αὐτῷ διαθήκην εἰρήνης ("therefore say thus: behold, I give him a covenant of peace").

**13** "And-it-shall-be [ve-hayetah] for-him [lo] and-for-his-seed [u-le-zar'o] after-him [akharav] a-covenant of [berit] priesthood of [kehunnat] perpetuity [olam], because [takhat] that [asher] he-was-zealous [qinne'] for-his-God [le-Elohav] and-he-atoned [va-yekhapper] for [al] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el]."

> **Key terms:** כְּהֻנַּת עוֹלָם (kehunnat olam) = "priesthood of perpetuity" — the eternal priestly covenant, ensuring that Phinehas's line would hold the high priesthood in perpetuity. Historically, this was understood as the foundation of the Zadokite priestly line (Ezek 40:46; 44:15; 1 Chr 5:30–34). תַּחַת אֲשֶׁר (takhat asher) = "because/in exchange for the fact that" — literally "under/in place of what," a causal conjunction. וַיְכַפֵּר (va-yekhapper) = "and-he-atoned" — from כפר (kappar, piel, "to cover/atone/make expiation"). Phinehas's act of killing is described with sacrificial-priestly language: his spear-thrust functions as an atoning act, turning back wrath as a sacrifice turns back judgment. This is the only instance in the Torah where a violent act by a human being is explicitly called "atonement." **LXX:** καὶ ἔσται αὐτῷ καὶ τῷ σπέρματι αὐτοῦ μετ᾽ αὐτὸν διαθήκη ἱερατείας αἰωνία, ἀνθ᾽ ὧν ἐζήλωσεν τῷ θεῷ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐξιλάσατο περὶ τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ ("and it shall be for him and for his seed after him a covenant of eternal priesthood, in return for which he was zealous for his God and made atonement for the sons of Israel").

**14** And-the-name of [ve-shem] the-man [ish] of-Israel [ha-Yisra'eli] the-one-struck [ha-mukkeh], who [asher] was-struck [hukkah] with [et] the-Midianite-woman [ha-Midyanit], [was] Zimri [Zimri] son of [ben] Salu [Salu], a-leader of [nesi] a-father's-house [beit av] of-the-Simeonites [la-Shim'oni].

> **Key terms:** הַמֻּכֶּה (ha-mukkeh) = "the one struck" — the hophal participle of נכה (nakah, "to strike"), i.e., the one who was slain. The double identification — אֲשֶׁר הֻכָּה אֶת הַמִּדְיָנִית (asher hukkah et ha-Midyanit) = "who was struck with the Midianite woman" — clarifies that both were killed together. זִמְרִי בֶּן סָלוּא (Zimri ben Salu) = "Zimri son of Salu" — the name זִמְרִי may derive from זמר (zamar, "to sing/prune"). He is identified as a נְשִׂיא בֵית אָב (nesi beit av) = "leader of a father's house" — a prominent clan chief within the tribe of Simeon. This elevated status intensifies the scandal: a tribal prince parading his Midianite consort before Moses. **LXX:** τὸ δὲ ὄνομα τοῦ ἀνθρώπου τοῦ Ισραηλίτου τοῦ πεπληγότος, ὃς ἐπλήγη μετὰ τῆς Μαδιανίτιδος, Ζαμβρι υἱὸς Σαλω, ἄρχων οἴκου πατριᾶς τῶν Συμεων ("and the name of the Israelite man who was struck, who was struck with the Midianite woman: Zambri son of Salo, ruler of a father's house of the Simeonites").

**15** And-the-name of [ve-shem] the-woman [ha-ishah] the-Midianite [ha-Midyanit] the-one-struck [ha-mukkah] [was] Cozbi [Kozbi] daughter of [bat] Zur [Tsur]; a-head of [rosh] peoples of [ummot] a-father's-house [beit av] in-Midian [be-Midyan] [was] he.

> **Key terms:** כׇּזְבִּי (Kozbi) = "Cozbi/Kozbi" — the name likely derives from כזב (kazav, "to lie/deceive"), meaning "my lie" or "my deception" — a fitting name in context, whether original or an editorial characterization. בַּת צוּר (bat Tsur) = "daughter of Zur" — this Zur is identified in Num 31:8 as one of the five kings of Midian slain in the subsequent war. רֹאשׁ אֻמּוֹת בֵּית אָב בְּמִדְיָן (rosh ummot beit av be-Midyan) = "head of peoples of a father's house in Midian" — Zur's rank is emphasized: he was a chief among the Midianite tribal confederacy. The pairing of the two victims — a Simeonite prince and a Midianite princess — underscores that this was no private tryst but a political-cultic alliance at the highest levels. **LXX:** καὶ ὄνομα τῇ γυναικὶ τῇ Μαδιανίτιδι τῇ πεπληγυίᾳ Χασβι θυγάτηρ Σουρ, ἄρχοντος ἔθνους Ομμωθ, οἴκου πατριᾶς ἐστιν τῶν Μαδιαν ("and the name of the Midianite woman who was struck: Chasbi daughter of Sour, ruler of the nation of Ommoth, of a father's house of Midian").

---

## The Command against Midian (vv. 16–18)

**16** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**17** "Bring-distress upon [tsaror] [et] the-Midianites [ha-Midyanim], and-you-shall-strike [ve-hikkitem] [et]-them [otam];

> **Key terms:** צָרוֹר אֶת הַמִּדְיָנִים (tsaror et ha-Midyanim) = "bring distress upon the Midianites" — the infinitive absolute of צרר (tsarar, "to be hostile/afflict/besiege"), functioning as an imperative. This root appeared in 10:9 (הַצַּר הַצֹּרֵר, "the adversary who oppresses"); now Israel is commanded to become the oppressor of Midian. The command is carried out in Numbers 31. **LXX:** ἔχθραινε τοῖς Μαδιηνίταις καὶ πατάξατε αὐτούς ("be hostile to the Midianites and strike them").

**18** "For [ki] they-are-bringing-distress upon [tsorerim] you [hem lakhem], in-their-schemes [be-nikhleihem] which [asher] they-schemed [nikkelu] against-you [lakhem] upon [al] the-matter of [devar] Peor [Pe'or], and-upon [ve-al] the-matter of [devar] Cozbi [Kozbi] daughter of [bat] a-leader of [nesi] Midian [Midyan], their-sister [akhotam], the-one-struck [ha-mukkah] on-the-day of [be-yom] the-plague [ha-maggefah] upon [al] the-matter of [devar] Peor [Pe'or]."

> **Key terms:** צֹרְרִים הֵם לָכֶם (tsorerim hem lakhem) = "they are bringing distress upon you" — the active participle of צרר, forming a chiastic echo with the imperative צָרוֹר (tsaror) in v. 17: "oppress them, for they are oppressing you." בְּנִכְלֵיהֶם אֲשֶׁר נִכְּלוּ לָכֶם (be-nikhleihem asher nikkelu lakhem) = "in their schemes which they schemed against you" — from נכל (nakhal, "to be crafty/to deceive"), a cognate accusative construction intensifying the deception. The verse identifies two specific instances of Midianite treachery: (1) עַל דְּבַר פְּעוֹר (al devar Pe'or) = "upon the matter of Peor" — the whole Baal Peor apostasy; and (2) עַל דְּבַר כׇּזְבִּי (al devar Kozbi) = "upon the matter of Cozbi" — the specific provocation of the Midianite princess. אֲחוֹתָם (akhotam) = "their sister" — identifying Cozbi as the Midianites' own kinswoman, deployed as an instrument of seduction. The chapter ends abruptly, with the execution of the command deferred to chapter 31. **LXX:** ὅτι ἐχθραίνουσιν αὐτοὶ ὑμῖν ἐν δολιότησιν, ὅσα δολιοῦσιν ὑμᾶς διὰ Φογωρ καὶ διὰ Χασβι θυγατέρα ἄρχοντος Μαδιαν ἀδελφὴν αὐτῶν τὴν πεπληγυῖαν ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῆς πληγῆς διὰ Φογωρ ("for they are hostile to you with deceit, as much as they deceive you on account of Phogor and on account of Chasbi daughter of a ruler of Midian, their sister, the one struck in the day of the plague on account of Phogor").

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (Pages 178–179)

1. **Column Layout and Section Breaks:** Numbers 25 appears in the standard three-column format of the Leningrad Codex. The text flows from the end of chapter 24 (Balaam's oracles) directly into the Shittim narrative. The critical parashah break between v. 9 and v. 10 is clearly marked with a פ (pe, open section), creating a significant visual gap — the largest within the chapter. This open section divides the Balak parashah from the Phinehas parashah.

2. **The Broken Vav of שָׁלוֹם (v. 12):** The Leningrad Codex preserves the tradition of the וָו קְטִיעָא (vav qeti'a, "broken/severed vav") in the word שָׁלוֹם in v. 12. This scribal tradition — in which the vertical stroke of the vav is intentionally broken or shortened — is one of the most discussed Masoretic anomalies. The Talmud (Qiddushin 66b) records awareness of this feature. Interpretive suggestions include: (a) that the covenant of peace was itself qualified or incomplete; (b) that the letter was damaged in an early manuscript and faithfully transmitted; (c) that it functions as a mnemonic for the paradox of violent peace.

3. **Hapax Legomenon — הַקֻּבָּה (v. 8):** The word קֻּבָּה ("tent-chamber") occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible. The Leningrad Codex vocalizes it with a qubbutz under the qoph, distinguishing it from קֵבָה (qevah, "stomach") in the same verse. The Masorah parva likely notes the uniqueness of this form.

4. **Ketiv/Qere — None Noted:** Numbers 25 does not contain any ketiv/qere variants in the Leningrad Codex, which is notable for a chapter of this narrative intensity. The text appears to have been transmitted with unusual stability.

### Comparison: Leningrad Codex (MT) vs. Codex Vaticanus (LXX)

| Verse | MT (Leningrad) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Significance |
|-------|----------------|------------------|--------------|
| v. 1 | וַיָּחֶל "and-began" | ἐβεβηλώθη "was profaned" | LXX selects the "profane" meaning of חלל rather than "begin" — an interpretive choice that front-loads the moral judgment |
| v. 3 | וַיִּצָּמֶד "and-yoked-himself" | ἐτελέσθη "was initiated" | LXX interprets the yoking metaphor as cultic initiation into the Baal Peor rites |
| v. 4 | וְהוֹקַע "and-hang/impale" | παραδειγμάτισον "make an example of" | LXX softens the physical specificity of the execution method |
| v. 8 | הַקֻּבָּה "the tent-chamber" | τὴν κάμινον "the vault/furnace" | LXX appears uncertain about this hapax, rendering with a word for an enclosed space |
| v. 8 | קֳבָתָהּ "her belly" | τῆς μήτρας αὐτῆς "her womb" | LXX makes the anatomical reference more explicit |
| v. 12 | בְּרִיתִי שָׁלוֹם "my covenant of peace" | διαθήκην εἰρήνης "covenant of peace" | Both traditions agree; LXX does not reflect the broken vav tradition |
| v. 18 | בְּנִכְלֵיהֶם "in their schemes" | ἐν δολιότησιν "in deceits" | Semantic equivalence; both emphasize Midianite craftiness |

### Theological Notes

1. **The Paradox of Violent Atonement:** Verse 13 applies the priestly verb כִּפֶּר (kipper, "to atone") to Phinehas's act of killing — the only instance in Torah where lethal human violence is explicitly called atonement. This challenges any neat separation between priestly sacrifice and judicial execution: Phinehas's spear functions as the priestly knife, the sinners as the sin-offering, and the plague's cessation as proof of divine acceptance.

2. **Yoking and Un-Yoking:** The root צמד (tsamad, "to yoke") structures the chapter. Israel "yokes itself" to Baal Peor (v. 3); the guilty are identified as "the ones yoked" (v. 5); and in the command against Midian, Israel is told to be "hostile" (צרר) — to unyoke itself violently from the foreign entanglement. The agricultural metaphor of yoking suggests a kind of covenant-bondage: to be yoked to Baal is to be unfaithful to YHWH, the true covenant partner.

3. **The Midianite Connection:** The chapter begins with "daughters of Moab" (v. 1) but shifts to a "Midianite woman" (v. 6) and concludes with a command against "Midian" (vv. 17–18). This Moab-Midian interchange reflects the geopolitical reality of overlapping territories and the narrative arc connecting Balak's failed curse (chs. 22–24) to Balaam's counsel (cf. Num 31:16, where Balaam is credited with advising the sexual-cultic strategy). The Midianites, not the Moabites, bear the primary blame and face military retribution.

4. **Twenty-Four Thousand (v. 9):** The number of plague dead — twenty-four thousand — is one of the largest single-event death tolls in the wilderness narratives, exceeded only by the plague after the spies (14:37, implied larger scope) and the Korah rebellion (17:14 [Eng. 16:49]: fourteen thousand seven hundred). Paul's citation of "twenty-three thousand" in 1 Cor 10:8 has generated extensive discussion; the most common harmonization distinguishes those who fell "in one day" (Paul) from the total including the judicially executed leaders.
